note: Foreign-born population figures come from the 2021/22 Census (Census 2021 for England, Wales and Northern Ireland; Census 2022 for Scotland). ONS discontinued its annual country-of-birth population series in October 2022, so there is no reliable official foreign-born total after mid-2021. A frequently cited '19% in 2024' appears in some secondary sources but is not a current accredited ONS estimate. Citizenship and settlement figures come from Home Office Immigration system statistics (year ending December 2025).

metrics.metric_name: Foreign-born (non-UK born) population of the UK
metrics.source_name: Migration Observatory (University of Oxford), Migrants in the UK: An Overview, based on the 2021/22 Census
metrics.notes: The most reliable available figure. The foreign-born population has continued to rise since 2021, but no reliable post-2021 official total exists.

metrics.metric_name: Foreign-born share of total UK population
metrics.source_name: Migration Observatory (University of Oxford), Migrants in the UK: An Overview
metrics.notes: Roughly one in six residents, of a total population of 66.9 million at the time. Up from about 9% in 2004. A frequently cited '19% in June 2024' figure is not a current accredited ONS estimate.

metrics.source_name: Migration Observatory (University of Oxford), Migrants in the UK: An Overview
metrics.notes: Foreign-citizen (non-British nationality) share, up from about 5% in 2004. Differs from the foreign-born group: many migrants naturalise, and some UK-born residents are non-citizens.

metrics.source_name: ONS, Population estimates for the UK: mid-2024 (released 26 September 2025)
metrics.notes: Latest official mid-year estimate (69.3 million). Up 755,300 (1.1%) on mid-2023, with net international migration of 738,700 the main driver. Accredited official statistics.

metrics.metric_name: Largest country of birth among foreign-born — India (share)
metrics.source_name: Migration Observatory (University of Oxford), Migrants in the UK: An Overview
metrics.notes: India was the largest single country of birth (9%). The top five — India (9%), Poland (8%), Pakistan (6%), Romania (5%), Ireland (4%) — together accounted for 32% of the foreign-born population.

metrics.source_name: Home Office, Immigration system statistics, year ending March 2026 — settlement and citizenship
metrics.notes: Down 12% on the year ending March 2025 (269,178). Naturalisation 165,429; registration 71,083. Applications rose 18% to 312,063 over the same period. Supersedes the year ending December 2025 figure of 235,782.

metrics.metric_name: Settlement (Indefinite Leave to Remain, excluding EU Settlement Scheme) grants
metrics.source_name: Home Office, Immigration system statistics, year ending March 2026 — settlement and citizenship
metrics.notes: Down 11% on the year ending March 2025 (170,237). Excludes EU Settlement Scheme settled status. Refugee settlement grants within this fell 53% to 19,960. Supersedes the year ending December 2025 figure of 146,405.

metrics.metric_name: EU Settlement Scheme settled status grants
metrics.source_name: Home Office, Immigration system statistics, year ending December 2025 — settlement and citizenship
metrics.notes: About 4.4 million settled-status grants have been issued since the scheme launched in 2018. Classified as Official Statistics (not accredited). Includes an estimated 86,670 automated grants.

metrics.metric_name: Share of foreign-born residents living in London and the South East
metrics.source_name: Migration Observatory (University of Oxford), Migrants in the UK: An Overview
metrics.notes: About 47% of foreign-born residents lived in London and the South East, against 27% of the overall population.

metrics.metric_name: Share of London residents born abroad
metrics.source_name: Migration Observatory (University of Oxford), Migrants in the UK: An Overview
metrics.notes: More than 40% of London residents were born abroad, the highest of any region. National average 16%. Lowest: North East (7%), Wales (7%), Northern Ireland (9%).
